result:
The Art of Getting Lost

There's something deeply human about the act of getting lost. Not the panicked, check-your-phone-battery kind of lost, but the deliberate wandering that our ancestors must have known intimately. The kind where you turn left instead of right simply because the light catches differently on that unfamiliar street, or because you heard music drifting from somewhere you've never been.

I've been thinking about this lately—how we've engineered the possibility of being lost right out of our lives. GPS coordinates pulse from our pockets, blue dots sliding along predetermined paths, arrival times calculated to the minute. We've traded mystery for efficiency, and while I wouldn't give up the ability to find the nearest emergency room at 2 AM, I wonder what else we've surrendered in the exchange.

The last time I truly got lost was in Lisbon, three years ago. My phone had died somewhere between the castle and the river, and I found myself in a maze of tilting streets with no familiar landmarks. The initial flutter of anxiety gave way to something else—a heightened attention to detail I hadn't experienced in years. Without the crutch of digital navigation, my senses sharpened. I noticed the way laundry flapped like prayer flags between buildings, how the afternoon light turned white walls to honey, the particular sound of Portuguese grandmothers calling to each other across narrow alleys.

I walked for hours, letting the city reveal itself in its own time. Down a street barely wide enough for two people to pass, I discovered a tiny tasca where locals nursed afternoon wines and the owner's cat supervised from atop the cash register. The menu was handwritten and incomprehensible, so I pointed at what the man next to me was eating—some kind of stew that turned out to be the best thing I'd eat all week. No TripAdvisor would have led me there. No algorithm would have suggested it based on my previous dining history.

This is what we lose when we optimize our paths: the gift of accident, the education of disorientation. Every wrong turn is a teacher. Every dead end forces us to lift our eyes from our screens and actually see where we are. The inefficiency is the point—it's in those wasted minutes and unnecessary miles that we stumble upon the unexpected.

Children understand this instinctively. Watch a child walk to school and you'll see them take the most circuitous route possible—balancing on walls, investigating puddles, following cats. They haven't yet learned that the shortest distance between two points is a straight line, or perhaps they know something we've forgotten: that the shortest distance between two points might be the least interesting.

There's a Japanese word, "tsundoku," which means acquiring books and letting them pile up without reading them. I think we need a similar word for the places we mean to visit but never do, the streets we pass every day but never turn down. Our cities are full of these parallel lives we're not living, these alternate routes we're not taking.

I've started practicing small acts of deliberate lostness. I get off the subway one stop early. I take streets I've never taken in neighborhoods I think I know. Last week, walking an unfamiliar route to a familiar destination, I passed a man teaching his daughter to ride a bicycle in an empty parking lot. "Keep pedaling!" he called out in Spanish, jogging alongside her wobbling progress. "Don't look down! Look where you want to go!" 

It struck me as perfect advice for more than just cycling. We spend so much time looking down—at our phones, at our feet, at the familiar ground we've covered a thousand times. But getting lost forces us to look up, to look forward, to engage with the world as it actually is rather than as we expect it to be.

Of course, there's privilege in choosing to get lost. Throughout history, being lost has more often been a crisis than an adventure—for refugees, for the enslaved seeking freedom, for those without homes. The luxury of wandering assumes you have somewhere to return to, that your lostness is temporary and voluntary. This isn't something to take lightly.

But for those of us who can afford to occasionally surrender our certainty, who can trade our efficiency for discovery, I think there's value in reclaiming this small wildness. In an age where every moment is scheduled and every location is mapped, choosing to not know where you're going becomes a quiet form of rebellion.

The city where I live now has become too familiar. I know which subway car to board so I'm closest to the exit at my stop. I know which coffee shop makes the best cortado and exactly how long it takes to walk there. This knowledge is comfortable, but comfort and discovery rarely share the same address.

So tomorrow, I think I'll get lost again. Not dramatically—just a small deviation from my routine geography. Maybe I'll finally turn down that side street I always pass, the one with the faded blue door and the window full of orchids. Maybe I'll find nothing more than another street beyond it. Or maybe I'll find a hidden garden, a perfect bench, a conversation with a stranger, a view I've never seen before.

The beauty is in not knowing. The gift is in the wandering itself, in saying yes to the possibility that around the next unfamiliar corner, the world might surprise you. In a time when surprises are increasingly rare, when algorithms predict our desires before we even know them ourselves, the simple act of getting lost might be the most radical thing we can do.

After all, every explorer was once just someone who didn't know where they were going.
